These are populated by `/consolidate-specs`. ## Skill Workflow ```mermaid graph LR A["/brainstorm
proposal.md"] --> B["/write-prd
prd/*.{3} + manifest.md"] B --> C["/eval-prd
eval report"] C -->|"has UI"| G["/ui-design

eval report"] E --> F["/breakdown-tasks
tasks/*.md + manifest.md"] ``` ```mermaid graph LR F["/breakdown-tasks
appends T-test-1..5"] --> T1["/gen-sitemap*
sitemap.json"] T1 --> T2["/gen-test-cases
test-cases.md"] T2 --> T2b["/eval-test-cases
eval report"] T2b --> T3["/gen-test-scripts
tests/e2e/features/"] T3 --> T4["/run-e2e-tests
results/"] T4 --> T4b["verify-regression
regression check"] T4b --> T5["/graduate-tests
tests/e2e/target/"] T5 --> T6["/consolidate-specs
specs/{biz,tech}-specs.md"] ``` > * /gen-sitemap is a prerequisite command (called by T-test-1), not a standalone T-test task. Each skill checks prerequisites with `ls` before execution; aborts and prompts user if missing. ### Quick Mode For small features (1-2h, 1-4 tasks), use the streamlined pipeline: ```mermaid graph LR Q0["/quick"] --> Q1["/brainstorm
proposal.md"] Q1 --> Q2{"human confirm"} Q2 -->|"yes"| Q3["/quick-tasks
tasks/*.md + index.json"] Q2 -->|"revise"| Q1 Q3 --> Q4["/run-tasks
auto-execute"] ``` **When to use Quick Mode:** - Feature is 1-2 hours of work - 1-4 tasks maximum - No complex architecture decisions needed - Proposal provides enough context (no PRD/design needed) **When to use Full Mode:** - Feature >2 hours or >4 tasks - Requires PRD with acceptance criteria - Needs tech design with architecture decisions - Has UI design requirements - Needs multi-phase execution with gates **Quick mode differences:** - No PRD, no design, no eval steps - `proposal.md` is the sole input document - Flat task list (no phases, no gates, no summaries) - T-quick-1~5 test tasks (subset of T-test 7 tasks: skips gen-sitemap prerequisite, eval-test-cases, consolidate-specs) - Simplified manifest (no Traceability table) - `--no-test` flag skips T-quick-1~5 ### Manifest `manifest.md` is the single entry point for a Feature. An AI agent reads this file to understand the full context: - **Documents** table: lists all document paths and auto-generated summaries - **Traceability** table: PRD ŌåÆ Design ŌåÆ Tasks mapping - **Status** (feature-level): prd ŌåÆ design ŌåÆ tasks ŌåÆ in-progress ŌåÆ completed - Not to be confused with task-level statuses in index.json: pending, in_progress, completed, blocked, skipped, rejected ## Quality Gate Protocol All task-executing workflows (`/execute-task`, `task-executor` agent, `/fix-bug`, `error-fixer` agent) MUST pass the quality gate before recording completion. Quality gate sequence: `just compile ŌåÆ just fmt ŌåÆ just lint ŌåÆ just test`. On failure: compile ŌåÆ fix & retry; fmt ŌåÆ blocked (toolchain issue); lint ŌåÆ self-fix (1 retry) then blocked; test ŌåÆ fix & retry. ### Scope Resolution Before each `just ` command, resolve scope from the task's `scope` field: 1. If `scope` is missing, empty, or `"all"` ŌåÆ `just ` (no scope argument). 2. If `scope` is `"frontend"` or `"backend"`: a. Run `just project-type`, capture stdout (trimmed) and exit code. b. If exit code != 0, or output not in `frontend`/`backend`/`mixed` ŌåÆ fallback to `just `. c. If output == `"mixed"` ŌåÆ `just `. d. If output is `"frontend"` or `"backend"` (not mixed) ŌåÆ fallback to `just `. ### All-Completed Hook After all tasks done, runs as final safety net (no scope ŌĆö project-wide): 1. Quality gate: `just compile ŌåÆ just fmt ŌåÆ just lint` 2. Project-wide tests: `just test` 3. E2E regression: `just e2e-setup ŌåÆ just probe ŌåÆ just test-e2e` On failure at any step, a P0 fix-task is automatically created. Run `task claim` to pick it up. ## Testing Lifecycle Three layers of testing, each with distinct purpose and trigger: | Layer | Command | Scope | When | |---|---|---|---| | Unit Tests | `just test [scope]` | Task-level | Every task verify step (Quality Gate) | | Feature E2E | `just test-e2e --feature ` | Feature-level | T-test-3 after scripts generated | | Regression Suite | `tests/e2e/` | Project-level | all-completed hook; graduated via T-test-4 | ``` Unit (per task) ŌöĆŌöĆŌåÆ Feature E2E (T-test-3) ŌöĆŌöĆŌåÆ Regression (graduate to tests/e2e/) Ōåæ Quality Gate enforces Ōåæ T-test-4 graduates ``` ## Task-CLI Task CLI manages task lifecycle within feature workflows. **Typical flow**: Before starting work, run `task feature` ŌåÆ `task claim` to get a task ŌåÆ `task record` to save results + update task status. ### Key Commands | Operation | Command | Description | |-----------|---------|-------------| | Switch feature | `task feature ` | Set current work context | | Claim task | `task claim` | Get next available task | | Complete task | `task record --data docs/features/{slug}/tasks/process/record.json` | **One step for record + status update** | | Add task | `task add --title "..." [flags]` | Add task to current feature | | Apply template | `task template ` | View task template content | > For record workflow details, see the `/record-task` skill. For full command reference, run `task -h` or `task [command] -h`. " } }

content.text:
# /run-tasks

Auto-dispatch tasks. MAIN_SESSION tasks execute in main session; all others go to forge:task-executor subagent.

## Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["1. Claim Task"] --> B{"MAIN_SESSION?"}
    B -->|"yes"| C["1.5 Follow Task Instructions"]
    C --> LOOP(["Step 6: Continue Loop"])
    B -->|"no"| D["2. Dispatch + Timeout"]
    D --> E["3. Verify Record"]
    E --> F["4. Context Check"]
    F --> G{"Breaking task?"}
    G -->|"yes"| H["5. Breaking Gate"]
    G -->|"no"| LOOP
    H --> LOOP
    LOOP --> A
```

## Dispatcher Iron Laws

<EXTREMELY-IMPORTANT>
1. Only 5 actions: claim ŌåÆ (main_session? follow task instructions : dispatch) ŌåÆ verify ŌåÆ context check ŌåÆ breaking gate
2. NO code reading, NO code writing ŌĆö EXCEPT for MAIN_SESSION tasks (Step 1.5) where the Skill tool is invoked in the main session
3. NO running tests directly ŌĆö EXCEPT in Step 5 (Breaking Task Gate) where `just test` and `just test-e2e` are executed as quality gates
4. 30-minute timeout per task
5. 3 consecutive failures ŌåÆ STOP
</EXTREMELY-IMPORTANT>

## Execution Loop

### Step 1: Claim Task

```bash
task claim
```

**Output parsing**:
- `ACTION: CLAIMED` ŌåÆ New task
- `ACTION: CONTINUE` ŌåÆ Resume existing task
- Error ŌåÆ No available task, end loop

**Extract from claim output**:
- `TASK_ID` (e.g., "2.1")
- `KEY` (e.g., "2.1-implementation")
- `FILE` (e.g., full absolute path to task file)
- `BREAKING` (e.g., "true" or absent)
- `MAIN_SESSION` (e.g., "true" or absent)
- `SCOPE` (e.g., "frontend", "backend", or "all" ŌĆö defaults to "all" if absent; may be omitted entirely by claim output when not set)
- `NO_TEST` (e.g., "true" or "false")
- `FEATURE` (e.g., "my-feature" ŌĆö feature slug from claim output)

### Step 1.5: Main Session Routing

If `MAIN_SESSION == "true"`:

1. Read the task file at `{{FILE}}` and find the `## Main Session Instructions` section.
2. Follow the instructions exactly ŌĆö the task document specifies what skill to invoke, how to check outcome, and how to record the result.
3. The dispatcher does NOT hardcode skill names or record logic ŌĆö it delegates to the task document.
4. If the task file lacks a `## Main Session Instructions` section, mark the task blocked and report: "MAIN_SESSION task missing Main Session Instructions section ŌĆö task document is incomplete".
5. After execution, verify the record file exists (same as Step 3 for subagent tasks).
6. Skip to Step 6 (Continue Loop).

Else:
- Proceed to Step 2 (Dispatch with Timeout).

### Step 2: Dispatch with Timeout

Determine if phase summary context should be injected:

**Phase boundary detection**:
1. From `TASK_ID`, extract the phase number (integer before first dot, e.g., "2.1" ŌåÆ phase 2)
2. If this is the first task of a new phase (phase number changed from previous task):
   - Check if previous phase's summary record exists: `docs/features/<slug>/tasks/records/<prev-phase>-summary.md`
   - If exists, include in dispatch prompt
   - Skip injection for gate tasks (ID ends with `.gate`) and phase summary tasks (ID ends with `.summary`)

```
Agent(
  subagent_type="forge:task-executor",
  prompt="TASK_KEY: {{KEY}}
TASK_ID: {{TASK_ID}}
TASK_FILE: {{FILE}}
SCOPE: {{SCOPE}}
NO_TEST: {{NO_TEST}}
{{PHASE_SUMMARY_SECTION}}

IMPORTANT: Do NOT claim or start any other tasks after completing this one. Stop after recording the task result."
)
```

Where `{{PHASE_SUMMARY_SECTION}}` is:
```
PHASE_SUMMARY: Read the following file for context from previous phases:
docs/features/<slug>/tasks/records/<prev-phase>-summary.md
```

Phase summaries follow a fixed 5-section structure (Tasks Completed, Key Decisions, Types & Interfaces Changed, Conventions Established, Deviations from Design). The task-executor is trained to parse these sections.

Or empty string if no phase summary exists.

**Timeout**: 30 minutes

### Step 3: Verify Record

Check if record file exists after agent completes. Then check the task's actual status via CLI:

```bash
task query <TASK_ID>
```

- If STATUS is not `"completed"`: task was auto-downgraded (e.g. test failures).
  Spawn fix task using `--block-source` to atomically block the source:
  ```bash
  task add --template fix-task --title "Fix: <failure>" \
    --source-task-id <TASK_ID> \
    --block-source \
    --description "<reason>"
  ```
  `task add` automatically deduplicates ŌĆö check output:
  - `ACTION: ADDED` ŌåÆ new fix task created, continue loop
  - `ACTION: SKIPPED` ŌåÆ active fix task already exists, continue loop
- Only proceed if STATUS is `"completed"`

### Step 4: Context Check

After verifying the record, check if the completed task was a phase summary task (ID ends with `.summary`):
- If yes: This phase's summary is now available for subsequent phases
- No additional action needed ŌĆö the summary will be injected on next phase boundary

### Step 5: Breaking Task Gate

Determine which gates to run based on claim output from Step 1:

| BREAKING=true? | SCOPE frontend\|all + specs exist? | Run 5a? | Run 5b? |
|----------------|-------------------------------------|---------|---------|
| Yes | No | Yes | No |
| No | Yes | No | Yes |
| Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| No | No | Skip Step 5 entirely | Skip Step 5 entirely |

If running both: execute 5a first. Only proceed to 5b if 5a passes.

#### 5a. Unit/Integration Gate (BREAKING: true)

```bash
# Pre-flight: verify justfile and test recipe exist
if [ ! -f justfile ] && [ ! -f Justfile ]; then
    echo "Error: justfile not found ŌĆö run /init-justfile first" >&2
    exit 1
fi
just --list 2>/dev/null | grep -q "^    test " || {
    echo "Error: 'test' recipe not found in justfile" >&2
    exit 1
}
```

```bash
just test [scope]
```

Apply the **Scope Resolution** protocol from the Forge Guide ŌĆö use the `SCOPE` extracted from the claim output in Step 1.

**If tests fail**:
- Run `task template fix-task` to view the template, then add fix task:
  ```bash
  task add --template fix-task --title "Fix: <failure>" \
    --source-task-id <TASK_ID> \
    --block-source \
    --var SOURCE_FILES="<affected paths>" \
    --var TEST_SCRIPT="<failing test>" \
    --var TEST_RESULTS="<results path>" \
    --description "<root cause>"
  ```
  **`--block-source`**: atomically sets source task to blocked before resolution, preserving the fix-chain model.
  **`--source-task-id` auto-resolves**: if `<TASK_ID>` is a **completed** fix-task, the CLI automatically resolves to the root blocked task. Always pass the current failing task's ID ŌĆö no manual chain tracing needed.
- Continue loop ŌĆö fix task (P0) will be claimed on next iteration
- Do NOT proceed to next task until fix task completes

**If tests pass**: if the routing table indicates 5b should also run (SCOPE frontend|all + specs exist), proceed to 5b. Otherwise continue to next iteration (Step 1).

#### 5b. Feature E2E Gate (SCOPE=frontend|all, specs exist)

<EXTREMELY-IMPORTANT>
The dispatcher evaluates SCOPE and FEATURE from Step 1 claim output BEFORE executing any bash commands below. If SCOPE is `backend` or FEATURE is empty, skip this entire section.
</EXTREMELY-IMPORTANT>

Pre-conditions (all must be true):
- SCOPE is `frontend` or `all` (defaults to "all" if absent from claim output)
- FEATURE is non-empty (always true after successful claim)
- Feature has e2e spec files: `tests/e2e/features/$FEATURE/` contains `.spec.ts` files
- `test-e2e` recipe exists in justfile

```bash
# Pre-flight: verify test-e2e recipe exists ŌĆö if missing, skip to next iteration
SKIP=""
just --list 2>/dev/null | grep -q "test-e2e" || { echo "Skip: test-e2e recipe not found"; SKIP=true; }

# Check if specs exist for this feature
if [ -z "$(ls "tests/e2e/features/$FEATURE/"*.spec.ts 2>/dev/null)" ]; then
    echo "Skip: no .spec.ts files in tests/e2e/features/$FEATURE/"
    SKIP=true
fi

# If pre-flights passed, run e2e
if [ -z "$SKIP" ]; then
    just e2e-setup
    just test-e2e --feature "$FEATURE"
fi
```

**If e2e fails**:
- Add fix task using the fix-task template:
  ```bash
  task add --template fix-task --title "Fix: <concise description>" \
    --source-task-id <TASK_ID> \
    --block-source \
    --var SOURCE_FILES="<affected source paths>" \
    --var TEST_SCRIPT="tests/e2e/features/$FEATURE/<failing-spec>.spec.ts" \
    --var TEST_RESULTS="tests/e2e/features/$FEATURE/results/latest.md" \
    --description "<root cause and context>"
  ```

**If e2e passes or pre-flight skipped**: continue to next iteration (Step 1)

### Step 6: Continue Loop

Return to Step 1.

## Error Handling

| Situation | Action |
|-----------|--------|
| No available task | End loop, print summary |
| Agent timeout | Mark blocked, continue next |
| Record missing | Dispatch error-fixer (include: "Use /record-task skill to create record") |
| 3 consecutive failures | STOP dispatcher |
| Breaking task tests fail (5a) | `task add --template fix-task --block-source`, continue loop |
| Feature e2e tests fail (5b) | `task add --template fix-task --block-source`, continue loop |
| Main session task fails | Follow error handling in task document's `### Error Handling` section; if missing, `task add --template fix-task --block-source`, continue loop |

### Error-Fixer Dispatch

When dispatching error-fixer for missing record, include explicit instruction:

```
Agent(
  subagent_type="forge:error-fixer",
  prompt="TASK_ID: {{TASK_ID}}
TASK_FILE: {{FILE}}
ERROR_MESSAGES: Missing task record
INSTRUCTION: Use /record-task skill to create the record (task record CLI is mandatory)"
)
```

## Post-Completion

After all tasks are completed (loop ends with "No available task"):

```
Print summary to user:
"All tasks completed. T-test-3, T-test-4, and T-test-4.5 in the task chain handle
e2e verification, graduation, and regression automatically."
```

If the feature's task index does not include T-test-3/T-test-4, suggest:
```
"Run `/run-e2e-tests` to verify against PRD acceptance criteria,
then `/graduate-tests` to migrate scripts to the regression suite."
```

Do NOT run e2e tests outside of the Breaking Task Gate (Step 5) ŌĆö the dispatcher only executes tests as quality gates.

## Related Commands

| Command | Usage |
|---------|-------|
| `/execute-task` | Manual single task |
| `/record-task` | Create record + update status |
| `/run-e2e-tests` | E2E verification against PRD |

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 ŌåÆ 4.6, 4.6 ŌåÆ 4.7, retired-model replacements).

## Overview

### Component Diagram

### Dependencies

## Interfaces

### Interface 1: SessionStats µē®Õ▒ĢÕŁŚµ«Ą

```go
// MCPServerStats holds aggregated stats for one MCP server.
type MCPServerStats struct {
    Total int            // sum of all tool call counts under this server
    Tools map[string]int // tool name ŌåÆ call count
}

// SessionStats (extended)
type SessionStats struct {
    // existing fields unchanged
    TotalDuration  time.Duration
    ToolCallCounts map[string]int
    ToolTimePcts   map[string]float64
    PeakStep       ToolCallSummary

    // new fields
    SkillCounts map[string]int            // skill name ŌåÆ call count

// parseSkillInput extracts the skill name from a Skill tool_use input JSON.
// Falls back to the first 20 chars of raw input if "skill" field is absent or malformed.

// parseMCPToolName splits "mcp__<server>__<tool>" into (server, tool).
// Returns ("", "") if the name does not match the pattern.
func parseMCPToolName(toolName string) (server, tool string)

// parseHookMarker returns the hook type name if the text contains a known hook marker,
// or "" if no known marker is found.
// Known markers: "PreToolUse", "PostToolUse", "Stop", "user-prompt-submit-hook".
// Angle brackets are stripped: "<user-prompt-submit-hook>" ŌåÆ "user-prompt-submit-hook".

// Returns "" (empty string) when all three stat maps are empty.

## Data Models

1	package parser
2	
3	import (
4		"time"
5	)
6	
7	// EntryType distinguishes the kind of content in a JSONL line.
8	type EntryType int
9	
10	const (
11		EntryToolUse    EntryType = iota // tool_use message
12		EntryToolResult                  // tool_result message
13		EntryThinking                    // thinking block
14		EntryMessage                     // regular message
15	)
16	
17	// AnomalyType classifies detected anomalies.
18	type AnomalyType int
19	
20	const (
21		AnomalySlow         AnomalyType = iota // tool call duration >= 30s
22		AnomalyUnauthorized                    // access to path outside project dir
23	)
24	
25	// Session represents a parsed JSONL session file.
26	type Session struct {
27		FilePath  string        // absolute path to JSONL file
28		Date      time.Time     // file modification time or first record time
29		ToolCount int           // total tool_use messages
30		Duration  time.Duration // first to last message
31		Turns     []Turn        // ordered turn list
32		Cwd       string        // working directory from first entry
33		Title     string        // first user message text (truncated)
34	}
35	
36	// Turn groups related entries within a session.
37	type Turn struct {
38		Index     int // 1-based turn number
39		StartTime time.Time
40		Duration  time.Duration
41		Entries   []TurnEntry // tool calls, thinking, messages within this turn
42	}
43	
44	// TurnEntry represents a single parsed JSONL line.
45	type TurnEntry struct {
46		Type       EntryType // tool_use, tool_result, thinking, message
47		LineNum    int       // JSONL line number (1-based)
48		ToolName   string    // for tool_use entries
49		Input      string    // raw tool_use.input JSON
50		Output     string    // raw tool_result content
51		ExitCode   *int      // Bash-specific (nil for non-Bash tools)
52		Duration   time.Duration
53		Thinking   string      // thinking block content
54		Anomaly    *Anomaly    // nil if normal
55		Children   []TurnEntry // for sub-agent expansion (future)
56		IsExpanded bool        // UI state: expanded/collapsed
57	}
58	
59	// Anomaly represents a detected issue in a tool call.
60	type Anomaly struct {
61		Type     AnomalyType
62		LineNum  int
63		ToolName string
64		Duration time.Duration
65		FilePath string   // for unauthorized: the out-of-project path
66		Context  []string // parent call chain
67	}
68	
69	// ToolCallSummary holds aggregated info about a single tool call.
70	type ToolCallSummary struct {
71		ToolName string
72		Duration time.Duration
73	}
74	
75	// MCPServerStats holds aggregated stats for one MCP server.
76	type MCPServerStats struct {
77		Total int            // sum of all tool call counts under this server
78		Tools map[string]int // tool name ŌåÆ call count
79	}
80	
81	// SessionStats holds computed statistics for a session.
82	type SessionStats struct {
83		TotalDuration  time.Duration
84		ToolCallCounts map[string]int     // tool name to count
85		ToolTimePcts   map[string]float64 // tool name to percentage (0-100)
86		PeakStep       ToolCallSummary    // single slowest tool call
87	
88		// custom tool stats
89		SkillCounts map[string]int             // skill name ŌåÆ call count
90		MCPServers  map[string]*MCPServerStats // server name ŌåÆ stats
91		HookCounts  map[string]int             // hook type ŌåÆ trigger count
92	}

# UI Design: õ╗¬ĶĪ©ńøśĶć¬Õ«Üõ╣ēÕĘźÕģĘń╗¤Ķ«ĪÕī║ÕØŚ

## Design System

**Platform**: Terminal TUI (Bubble Tea + lipgloss)

**Color Tokens**

| Token | Value | Usage |
|-------|-------|-------|
| `fg-primary` | `"15"` (bright white) | Section titles, bold labels |
| `fg-secondary` | `"252"` (light gray) | Normal text, tool names |
| `fg-dim` | `"242"` (dim gray) | Indented sub-items, MCP tool rows |
| `fg-accent` | `"51"` (cyan) | Column headers |
| `fg-muted` | `"240"` (dark gray) | `(none)` placeholder, truncation hint |
| `bg-none` | ŌĆö | No background (transparent) |

**Typography**

- Section title: `lipgloss.NewStyle().Bold(true).Foreground("15")`
- Column header: `lipgloss.NewStyle().Foreground("51")`
- Normal row: `lipgloss.NewStyle().Foreground("252")`
- Sub-item row (MCP tool): `lipgloss.NewStyle().Foreground("242")` + 2-space indent
- Placeholder: `lipgloss.NewStyle().Foreground("240")`

**Layout**

- Column separator: 3 spaces (`   `)
- Sub-item indent: 2 spaces (`  `)
- Name column width: 22 chars (truncated with `ŌĆ”` if longer)
- Count column width: right-aligned, 4 chars

---

352				return i
353			}
354		}
355		return -1

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /Users/fanhuifeng/.claude/plugins/cache/forge/forge/2.18.0/skills/forensic # Forensic Agent Deviation Analysis ## When to Use **Trigger:** - User reports a skill/agent repeatedly deviating from expected behavior - User asks "why did the agent do X" about a past session - User wants to compare agent behavior across sessions for a specific skill - User provides `/forensic` command with a search term **Skip:** - Single-session post-mortem (use `/learn-lesson` instead) - Current-session debugging (investigate directly) ## Parameters | Parameter | Default | Description | | ------------- | ------- | ---------------------------------------------- | | `--keyword` | ŌĆö | Search history.jsonl for sessions with keyword | | `--session` | ŌĆö | Analyze a specific session ID | | `--skill` | ŌĆö | Analyze sessions that invoked a specific skill | | `--last` | 10 | Limit number of sessions to search | | `--target` | ŌĆö | Specific behavior to investigate (e.g. "agent ignored MAIN_SESSION flag") | ## Prerequisites `task` CLI must be installed with forensic subcommand (v2.15.0+). Verify: ```bash task forensic --help ``` If missing, build and install: `cd task-cli && go build -o ~/.zcode-task-cli/task ./cmd/task/` ## Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A["1. Search
history.jsonl"] --> B["2. Extract
evidence JSON"] B --> C["3. Load
skill definitions"] C --> D["4. Analyze
deviations"] D --> E["5. Report
docs/forensics/"] B --> B1["subagents
transcripts"] ``` ## Workflow After completing each workflow step, report its elapsed time in the format `[Step N: ]`. This ensures the user can see progress even when analysis takes several minutes. Example output after each step: ``` [Step 1: 3.2s] Found 4 sessions matching "init-justfile" [Step 2: 12.1s] Extracted evidence from 2 sessions (session-abc123: 8.4s, session-def456: 3.7s) [Step 3: 0.8s] Loaded 3 skill definitions [Step 4: 45.3s] Analyzed deviations across 2 sessions [Step 5: 1.2s] Report written to docs/forensics/init-justfile-deviation/report.md ``` ### Step 1: Locate Target Sessions Search `~/.claude/history.jsonl` to find relevant sessions. ```bash task forensic search "coding-harness/forge" --keyword "" --last ``` Or search by skill name: ```bash task forensic search "coding-harness/forge" --skill "" --last ``` From the results, identify sessions of interest. Read the `firstMsg` and `dateTime` to select relevant sessions. Present the session list to the user and confirm which sessions to analyze. ### Step 2: Extract Evidence For each confirmed session, extract compact evidence: ```bash # Derive JSONL path from sessionId # Path pattern: ~/.claude/projects/-Users-fanhuifeng-Projects-ai-coding-harness-forge/.jsonl mkdir -p docs/forensics//evidence task forensic extract ~/.claude/projects/-Users-fanhuifeng-Projects-ai-coding-harness-forge/.jsonl --out docs/forensics//evidence ``` Then check for subagent transcripts: ```bash task forensic subagents ~/.claude/projects/-Users-fanhuifeng-Projects-ai-coding-harness-forge/ ``` If subagents exist, extract their evidence too: ```bash task forensic extract --out docs/forensics//evidence ``` Evidence files are intermediate artifacts. Each extract produces ~10-20KB regardless of original JSONL size. Always use `--out` to write to the evidence directory ŌĆö never dump raw JSONL to terminal. ### Step 3: Load Expected Behavior From the evidence's `skillsUsed` field (or user's `--skill` parameter), read the relevant skill definitions: ``` plugins/forge/skills//SKILL.md ``` Extract the rules that the agent should have followed: - `` blocks ŌĆö mandatory constraints - `` blocks ŌĆö top-priority rules - `` blocks ŌĆö forbidden actions - Step-by-step workflow ŌĆö expected behavior sequence If `skillsUsed` is empty in the evidence (user messages may not always trigger skill detection), ask the user which skills were involved, or infer from the `--skill` parameter and the tool call patterns in the evidence. ### Step 4: Analyze Deviations Read the extracted evidence JSON from `docs/forensics//evidence/evidence.json`. **Proactively display timing data from the evidence.** The `summary.topSlowest` and `summary.timingByTool` fields contain per-action timing. When analyzing a session where the agent appeared stuck or inactive, present the timing breakdown prominently: ``` ## Timing Breakdown (session-abc123) | Action | Duration | Detail | |--------|----------|--------| | Agent (doc-scorer) | 142.3s | eval-design scoring... | | Read | 0.8s | plugins/forge/skills/... | | Bash | 45.2s | go test -race -cover ./... | | Edit | 0.3s | internal/cmd/forensic.go | Total tool time: 188.6s / Session duration: 12.4min ``` This helps the user immediately see where the agent spent time ŌĆö especially when investigating "stuck" tasks where one long-running tool call (often Agent/Bash) dominates. For each session, trace through: 1. **Thinking chain** ŌĆö Read each thinking block in order. Identify where reasoning diverges from the skill definition's expected workflow. 2. **Tool call sequence** ŌĆö Compare the actual tool calls against what the skill steps prescribe. Flag: - Tools called out of prescribed order - Tools called that aren't in the workflow - Required tools that were skipped 3. **Decision points** ŌĆö Where the thinking block shows a choice was made, check if the skill definition prescribes a specific choice. Flag deviations. Trace the causal chain at least 3 levels deep: 1. Symptom: What went wrong (observable behavior) 2. Direct cause: Which specific action/decision caused it 3. Root cause: Why the agent made that decision (instruction gap, context missing, wrong assumption) **Deviation categories** (use these to classify each finding): | Category | Description | Example | |----------|-------------|---------| | `instruction-gap` | Skill definition missing a critical rule | No instruction to handle MAIN_SESSION flag | | `context-starvation` | Agent lacked necessary information | Agent didn't see the record.json content | | `trust-without-verify` | Agent trusted its own output | Marked AC as met without running the artifact | | `wrong-priority` | Agent followed wrong priority | Chose "efficiency" over "safety" | | `scope-creep` | Agent exceeded its defined scope | Task executor claimed multiple tasks | | `pipeline-gap` | No enforcement between stages | Dispatcher checked file existence, not content | ### Step 5: Generate Report Write the forensic report using the template at `plugins/forge/skills/forensic/templates/report.md`. Output to: `docs/forensics//report.md` Present the report to the user. Do NOT commit automatically ŌĆö forensic reports are analysis artifacts that require human review. ## Common Mistakes - **Don't read raw JSONL files** ŌĆö Always use `task forensic extract` to compress the data. Raw JSONL is too large for analysis. - **Don't analyze a single data point** ŌĆö Cross-reference thinking blocks with the corresponding tool calls to understand the full decision chain. - **Don't skip the skill definition** ŌĆö You cannot identify deviations without knowing what the expected behavior was. - **Don't confuse symptom with root cause** ŌĆö "Agent recorded failed task as completed" is a symptom. "CLI validation accepts completed+testsFailed>0" is the root cause. ARGUMENTS: Õć║ńÄ░õ║åõ╗Ćõ╣łķŚ«ķóś’╝¤õĖ║õ╗Ćõ╣łÕü£µŁóõ║åõ╗╗ÕŖĪÕŠ¬ńÄ»’╝¤

content.thinking:
The user wants me to continue the forensic analysis. I now have all the evidence I need. Let me analyze what happened and write the report.

Key findings from the evidence:

1. **The task loop stopped at task 2.1** - The `forge:task-executor` subagent was dispatched 4 times for task 2.1 and each time it stopped mid-execution without completing.

2. **Pattern of subagent failures**:
   - Attempt 1 (line 62, 140.7s): Stopped at "Step 1/5: Reading task definition..." after 7 tool uses
   - Attempt 2 (line 76, 250.5s): Stopped at "Step 2/5: TDD implementation..." after 13 tool uses  
   - Attempt 3 (line 83, 190.3s): Stopped at "Step 1/5: Reading task definition... DONE" after 16 tool uses
   - Attempt 4 (line 99, 140.6s): Stopped at "Step 1/5: Reading task definition..." after 9 tool uses

3. **The dispatcher's response**: After 4 failures, the dispatcher (main session) correctly identified the pattern and decided to implement the task directly in the main session. It read all the reference files (dashboard.go, tech-design.md, ui-design.md, types.go, stats.go, dashboard_test.go) and was in the process of implementing when the user invoked `/forge:forensic`.

4. **Why did the subagent stop?** Looking at the evidence:
   - The subagent returned partial output like "Step 1/5: Reading task definition..." - this is the subagent's text output, not a tool call result
   - The subagent stopped with `stopReason: "end_turn"` in some cases - meaning it finished its turn without completing the task
   - The task is estimated at 3h and is complex (implementing renderCustomToolsBlock with 3 sub-functions, tests, etc.)

The root cause is likely one of:
1. **Context window exhaustion in subagent**: The task-executor subagent reads many reference files (dashboard.go, tech-design.md, ui-design.md, prototype HTML files, etc.) and then tries to write tests + implementation. This could exhaust the subagent's context window before it can complete.
2. **Output truncation**: The subagent's output was truncated by the API, causing the dispatcher to see only partial output
3. **Subagent hitting token limits**: The task requires reading 5+ large reference files plus writing substantial Go code

Looking at the thinking blocks more carefully:
- Line 130 thinking (101.7s, `end_turn`): "The subagent keeps stopping mid-execution. I need to implement task 2.1 directly in the main session..."
- This was the main session's thinking that ended with `end_turn` - meaning the main session itself stopped without writing any code!

So there are TWO problems:
1. The subagent kept stopping mid-execution (4 times)
2. The main session itself stopped after deciding to implement directly (line 130, end_turn) - it thought about the implementation for 101 seconds but then ended the turn without actually writing any code

The main session's `end_turn` at line 130 is the actual reason the task loop stopped. The main session was in the middle of implementing task 2.1 directly, had a 101-second thinking block, and then ended the turn without writing any code.

**Root cause analysis**:

1. **Subagent failures** (category: `context-starvation`):
   - Task 2.1 requires reading 5+ large reference files (dashboard.go ~373 lines, tech-design.md ~230 lines, ui-design.md ~100+ lines, 3 prototype HTML files, types.go, stats.go) before writing tests and implementation
   - The subagent's context fills up during the reading phase, leaving insufficient context for the actual implementation
   - The subagent returns partial output ("Step 1/5: Reading task definition...") because it ran out of context mid-execution

2. **Main session end_turn** (category: `instruction-gap`):
   - The run-tasks skill has no instruction for what to do when the main session itself decides to implement a task directly

Let me analyze the evidence and write the forensic report. The key findings from the evidence are:

1. The `forge:task-executor` subagent for task 2.1 stopped mid-execution 4 times without completing
2. The main session dispatcher (run-tasks) then tried to implement the task directly but also stopped (end_turn at line 130 with 101.7s thinking but no tool calls after)

Let me trace the causal chain:

**Finding 1: forge:task-executor subagent stops mid-execution on large tasks**

The subagent was dispatched 4 times for task 2.1 (estimated 3h). Each time it stopped after only a few tool calls:
- Attempt 1 (140.7s): stopped at "Step 1/5: Reading task definition..." after 7 tool uses
- Attempt 2 (250.5s): stopped at "Step 2/5: TDD implementation..." after 13 tool uses  
- Attempt 3 (190.3s): stopped at "Step 1/5: Reading task definition... DONE" after 16 tool uses
- Attempt 4 (140.6s): stopped at "Step 1/5: Reading task definition..." after 9 tool uses

The subagent returns a result string like "Step 1/5: Reading task definition..." which is just the last text output before it stopped. This is the subagent hitting its output limit or context limit and returning early.

The root cause: The `forge:task-executor` subagent is hitting the API's output token limit or context limit. When a subagent runs out of output tokens, it returns whatever it has so far. The task is large (3h estimated, complex Go implementation with TDD), requiring many file reads, writes, and test runs.

**Finding 2: run-tasks dispatcher lacks a "3 consecutive failures ŌåÆ STOP" enforcement**

The run-tasks skill says: "3 consecutive failures ŌåÆ STOP" in the error handling table. But the dispatcher kept retrying (4 times) instead of stopping after 3. 

Wait, looking more carefully - the dispatcher DID try 4 times. The rule says "3 consecutive failures ŌåÆ STOP". After the 3rd failure, the dispatcher should have stopped. Instead it tried a 4th time. This is a deviation.

Actually wait - let me re-read. The dispatcher tried:
1. First dispatch (line 62) - stopped
2. Second dispatch (line 76) - stopped  
3. Third dispatch (line 83) - stopped
4. Fourth dispatch (line 99) - stopped

After the 3rd failure, the dispatcher should have stopped per the "3 consecutive failures ŌåÆ STOP" rule. Instead it dispatched a 4th time.

**Finding 3: Main session attempted implementation but produced end_turn without writing code**

After 4 failed subagent dispatches, the main session decided to implement the task directly. It read all the reference files (dashboard.go, tech-design.md, ui-design.md, types.go, stats.go, dashboard_test.go) and then had a 101.7s thinking block that ended with `end_turn` - meaning it planned the implementation in its thinking but never actually called Write/Edit tools to create the code.

This is the critical failure that stopped the loop. The session ended (end_turn) without:
1. Writing any implementation code
2. Recording the task
3. Continuing the loop

The root cause here is that the main session's thinking block at line 130 planned the implementation but the response ended without any tool calls. This is likely because the thinking block consumed so much of the output budget that there was no room left for actual tool calls, OR the model decided to stop and present the plan to the user rather than executing it.

**Finding 4: run-tasks dispatcher has no fallback for persistent subagent failures**

The skill definition says to dispatch error-fixer when record is missing: "Dispatch error-fixer (include: 'Use /record-task skill to create record')". But the dispatcher instead kept re-dispatching the same task-executor. The error-fixer path was never taken.

Actually looking at the thinking blocks more carefully:
- Line 81: "I need to continue the agent or re-dispatch it" - re-dispatched task-executor
- Line 85: "This might be a context/complexity issue with the task" - re-dispatched again
- Line 94: "Let me read the task file to understand what's needed, then dispatch the error-fixer or try a different approach" - but then dispatched task-executor again (line 99)
- Line 101: "The subagent keeps stopping early after just reading the task file" - decided to implement directly

So the dispatcher:
1. Never dispatched error-fixer (despite the error handling table saying to do so for missing records)
2. Kept re-dispatching task-executor (violating the 3-failure STOP rule)
3. Attempted main-session implementation (not prescribed by the skill for non-MAIN_SESSION tasks)
4. The main-session implementation ended with end_turn without writing code

Let me now write the report.

# Task Loop Stopped at Task 2.1 ŌĆö Subagent Early Exit + Dispatcher Rule Violations

## Executive Summary

The `forge:run-tasks` dispatcher stalled on task 2.1 (`renderCustomToolsBlock`, estimated 3h) because the `forge:task-executor` subagent exited early 4 times without completing. The dispatcher violated two of its own iron laws: it retried past the 3-failure STOP threshold, never invoked the prescribed error-fixer fallback, and ultimately attempted a main-session implementation that ended with `end_turn` without writing any code ŌĆö leaving the loop permanently stuck.

## Investigation Scope

| Dimension | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Sessions analyzed | 1 |
| Time range | 2026-05-11 13:07 ŌåÆ 2026-05-12 01:21 (12.2h) |
| Skills involved | `forge:run-tasks`, `forge:task-executor` |
| Trigger | User reported task loop stopped; `/forge:forensic` invoked |

## Timing Overview

| Session | Duration | Tool Time | Idle | Top Bottleneck |
|---------|----------|-----------|------|----------------|
| 9ecc0bbe | 12.2h | 1456.5s | ~42.5h equiv | `Agent` (366.1s) |

| Tool | Calls | Total | Avg | Max |
|------|-------|-------|-----|-----|
| `Agent` | 8 | 1454.5s | 181.8s | 366.1s |
| `Bash` | 20 | 1.8s | 89ms | 722ms |
| `Read` | 7 | 152ms | 22ms | 27ms |

**Task 2.1 subagent attempts:**

| Attempt | Duration | Stopped at | Tool calls |
|---------|----------|------------|------------|
| 1 | 140.7s | "Step 1/5: Reading task definition..." | ~7 |
| 2 | 250.5s | "Step 2/5: TDD implementation..." | ~13 |
| 3 | 190.3s | "Step 1/5: Reading task definition... DONE" | ~16 |
| 4 | 140.6s | "Step 1/5: Reading task definition..." | ~9 |

---

## Findings

### Finding 1: forge:task-executor exits early on large tasks without completing

**Category:** `context-starvation`

**Affected sessions:** 9ecc0bbe (task 2.1, all 4 attempts)

**Symptom:**
The subagent returned after only 7ŌĆō16 tool calls, outputting only a partial step banner like `"Step 1/5: Reading task definition..."` with no record file created and task status still `in_progress`.

**Agent reasoning (from thinking block, line 85):**
> "The subagent is stopping after just a few steps without completing the task. This might be a context/complexity issue with the task."

**Expected behavior (from skill definition):**
The `forge:task-executor` is expected to complete all 5 steps (read ŌåÆ TDD ŌåÆ implement ŌåÆ test ŌåÆ record) and call `/record-task` before returning.

**Gap:**
Task 2.1 is a 3h implementation task requiring reading 6+ reference files, writing tests, implementing 4 Go functions, running `just test`, and recording. The subagent hits its output token budget before completing step 2. When the API output limit is reached, the subagent returns whatever text it has produced ŌĆö which is just the step banner. There is no mechanism in the task-executor to detect this and resume.

**Causal chain:**
1. **Symptom:** Subagent returns "Step 1/5..." without completing the task
2. **Direct cause:** Subagent exhausts output token budget mid-execution on a large task
3. **Root cause:** No output-budget awareness in `forge:task-executor`; no resume/checkpoint mechanism; task size (3h) exceeds what a single subagent invocation can reliably complete

---

### Finding 2: Dispatcher retried past the 3-failure STOP threshold

**Category:** `instruction-gap`

**Affected sessions:** 9ecc0bbe

**Symptom:**
The dispatcher dispatched `forge:task-executor` for task 2.1 four times. The run-tasks skill explicitly states: "3 consecutive failures ŌåÆ STOP dispatcher."

**Agent reasoning (from thinking block, line 81):**
> "I need to continue the agent or re-dispatch it."

And at line 94:
> "Let me read the task file to understand what's needed, then dispatch the error-fixer or try a different approach."

But then dispatched task-executor a 4th time (line 99) instead of stopping or switching to error-fixer.

**Expected behavior (from skill definition):**
```
| 3 consecutive failures | STOP dispatcher |
```

**Gap:**
The dispatcher's thinking correctly identified the failure pattern after attempt 3 but rationalized a 4th retry ("with clearer instructions"). The STOP rule has no enforcement mechanism ŌĆö it relies entirely on the dispatcher's self-discipline, which broke down under the pressure of wanting to complete the task.

**Causal chain:**
1. **Symptom:** 4 dispatches instead of stopping at 3
2. **Direct cause:** Dispatcher chose to retry with "clearer instructions" rather than stopping
3. **Root cause:** The 3-failure STOP rule is advisory text with no hard enforcement; the dispatcher can rationalize past it

---

### Finding 3: Error-fixer fallback was never invoked

**Category:** `pipeline-gap`

**Affected sessions:** 9ecc0bbe

**Symptom:**
After each failed attempt, the dispatcher checked for the missing record file and confirmed the task was still `in_progress`, but never dispatched `forge:error-fixer`.

**Expected behavior (from skill definition):**
```
| Record missing | Dispatch error-fixer (include: "Use /record-task skill to create record") |
```

**Agent reasoning (from thinking block, line 74):**
> "The record for task 2.1 is missing and the task is still in_progress. I need to re-dispatch the task-executor subagent to complete task 2.1."

**Gap:**
The dispatcher correctly detected the "record missing" condition but chose to re-dispatch task-executor instead of error-fixer. The error handling table maps "record missing" ŌåÆ error-fixer, but the dispatcher interpreted the situation as "task not done yet" rather than "task failed to record" ŌĆö a subtle but critical distinction.

**Causal chain:**
1. **Symptom:** Error-fixer never dispatched despite missing record
2. **Direct cause:** Dispatcher classified the situation as "needs retry" not "needs error recovery"
3. **Root cause:** The error handling table condition "record missing" is ambiguous ŌĆö it applies both to "subagent completed but forgot to record" and "subagent never completed"; the dispatcher chose the wrong branch

---

### Finding 4: Main-session implementation attempt ended with end_turn without writing code

**Category:** `trust-without-verify`

**Affected sessions:** 9ecc0bbe (line 130, thinking 101.7s ŌåÆ end_turn)

**Symptom:**
After 4 failed subagent dispatches, the dispatcher decided to implement task 2.1 directly in the main session. It read all 6 reference files (dashboard.go, tech-design.md, ui-design.md, types.go, stats.go, dashboard_test.go) and then had a 101.7-second thinking block that ended with `end_turn` ŌĆö no Write or Edit tool calls were made. The loop stopped permanently.

**Agent reasoning (from thinking block, line 130):**
> "The subagent keeps stopping mid-execution. I need to implement task 2.1 directly in the main session... Let me plan the implementation..."

The thinking block contains a detailed implementation plan but the response ended without executing it.

**Expected behavior:**
The run-tasks skill does not prescribe main-session implementation for non-`MAIN_SESSION` tasks. The only prescribed fallback for persistent failures is STOP. If the dispatcher chose to implement directly, it should have called Write/Edit tools ŌĆö not just planned in thinking.

**Gap:**
The 101.7s thinking block consumed the output budget planning the implementation. When the model finished thinking, it produced `end_turn` (no tool calls) ŌĆö likely because the thinking was so long that the model treated it as the response itself, or the output token budget was exhausted by the thinking. This is the proximate cause of the loop stopping: the session ended without any action.

**Causal chain:**
1. **Symptom:** Loop stopped permanently; no code written; task still in_progress
2. **Direct cause:** 101.7s thinking block ended with `end_turn` instead of tool calls
3. **Root cause:** Extended thinking consumed output budget; model treated the plan as the deliverable rather than executing it

---

## Cross-Session Patterns

| Pattern | Sessions | Category |
|---------|----------|----------|
| Subagent exits early on tasks estimated >2h | 9ecc0bbe (task 2.1 ├Ś4) | `context-starvation` |
| Dispatcher retries past STOP threshold when task is "almost done" | 9ecc0bbe | `instruction-gap` |

---

## Recommendations

| Priority | Action | Target File | Finding |
|----------|--------|-------------|---------|
| P0 | Add hard retry counter to dispatcher: after 3 failures on same task, call `task add --template fix-task --block-source` and break the retry loop ŌĆö no 4th attempt | `plugins/forge/skills/run-tasks/SKILL.md` | Finding 2 |
| P0 | Clarify error-fixer trigger: "record missing after subagent returns" ŌåÆ always dispatch error-fixer, regardless of whether the task appears incomplete | `plugins/forge/skills/run-tasks/SKILL.md` | Finding 3 |
| P1 | Add task size guard: if `estimated_time` Ōēź 2h, split into subtasks before dispatching, or use `SendMessage` to continue an existing subagent rather than spawning fresh | `plugins/forge/skills/run-tasks/SKILL.md` | Finding 1 |
| P1 | Add post-thinking action check: after any thinking block >30s, the next action must be a tool call ŌĆö if the model is about to end_turn after long thinking, it should instead call Write with the planned content | `plugins/forge/skills/run-tasks/SKILL.md` | Finding 4 |
| P2 | Add `MAIN_SESSION: true` to tasks estimated >2h so the dispatcher executes them in the main session (which has full context and no subagent output limits) | Task definition for 2.1 and similar large tasks | Finding 1 |

---

## Evidence

Evidence files at: `docs/forensics/task-loop-stop/evidence/`

| File | Source | Size |
|------|--------|------|
| evidence.json | Main session 9ecc0bbe | ~45 KB |

3. **Root cause:** The error handling table condition "record missing" is ambiguous ŌĆö it applies both to "subagent completed but forgot to record" and "subagent never completed"; the dispatcher chose the wrong branch

---

### Finding 4: Main-session implementation attempt ended with end_turn without writing code

**Category:** `trust-without-verify`

**Affected sessions:** 9ecc0bbe (line 130, thinking 101.7s ŌåÆ end_turn)

**Symptom:**
After 4 failed subagent dispatches, the dispatcher decided to implement task 2.1 directly in the main session. It read all 6 reference files (dashboard.go, tech-design.md, ui-design.md, types.go, stats.go, dashboard_test.go) and then had a 101.7-second thinking block that ended with `end_turn` ŌĆö no Write or Edit tool calls were made. The loop stopped permanently.

**Agent reasoning (from thinking block, line 130):**
> "The subagent keeps stopping mid-execution. I need to implement task 2.1 directly in the main session... Let me plan the implementation..."

The thinking block contains a detailed implementation plan but the response ended without executing it.

**Expected behavior:**
The run-tasks skill does not prescribe main-session implementation for non-`MAIN_SESSION` tasks. The only prescribed fallback for persistent failures is STOP. If the dispatcher chose to implement directly, it should have called Write/Edit tools ŌĆö not just planned in thinking.

**Gap:**
The 101.7s thinking block consumed the output budget planning the implementation. When the model finished thinking, it produced `end_turn` (no tool calls) ŌĆö likely because the thinking was so long that the model treated it as the response itself, or the output token budget was exhausted by the thinking. This is the proximate cause of the loop stopping: the session ended without any action.

**Causal chain:**
1. **Symptom:** Loop stopped permanently; no code written; task still in_progress
2. **Direct cause:** 101.7s thinking block ended with `end_turn` instead of tool calls
3. **Root cause:** Extended thinking consumed output budget; model treated the plan as the deliverable rather than executing it

---

## Cross-Session Patterns

| Pattern | Sessions | Category |
|---------|----------|----------|
| Subagent exits early on tasks estimated >2h | 9ecc0bbe (task 2.1 ├Ś4) | `context-starvation` |
| Dispatcher retries past STOP threshold when task is "almost done" | 9ecc0bbe | `instruction-gap` |

---

## Recommendations

| Priority | Action | Target File | Finding |
|----------|--------|-------------|---------|
| P0 | Add hard retry counter to dispatcher: after 3 failures on same task, call `task add --template fix-task --block-source` and break the retry loop ŌĆö no 4th attempt | `plugins/forge/skills/run-tasks/SKILL.md` | Finding 2 |
| P0 | Clarify error-fixer trigger: "record missing after subagent returns" ŌåÆ always dispatch error-fixer, regardless of whether the task appears incomplete | `plugins/forge/skills/run-tasks/SKILL.md` | Finding 3 |
| P1 | Add task size guard: if `estimated_time` Ōēź 2h, split into subtasks before dispatching, or use `SendMessage` to continue an existing subagent rather than spawning fresh | `plugins/forge/skills/run-tasks/SKILL.md` | Finding 1 |
| P1 | Add post-thinking action check: after any thinking block >30s, the next action must be a tool call ŌĆö if the model is about to end_turn after long thinking, it should instead call Write with the planned content | `plugins/forge/skills/run-tasks/SKILL.md` | Finding 4 |
| P2 | Add `MAIN_SESSION: true` to tasks estimated >2h so the dispatcher executes them in the main session (which has full context and no subagent output limits) | Task definition for 2.1 and similar large tasks | Finding 1 |

---

## Evidence

Evidence files at: `docs/forensics/task-loop-stop/evidence/`

| File | Source | Size |
|------|--------|------|
| evidence.json | Main session 9ecc0bbe | ~45 KB |

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /Users/fanhuifeng/.claude/plugins/cache/forge/forge/2.18.0/skills/record-task

# Record Task

## Overview

Post-task completion: create execution record + update task status.

## File Locations

| Location                                         | Purpose                     | Git Status        |
| ------------------------------------------------ | --------------------------- | ----------------- |
| `docs/features/{slug}/tasks/process/record.json` | In-progress execution notes | Not committed     |
| `docs/features/{slug}/tasks/records/*.md`        | Final completed record      | Committed to repo |

**Workflow:**

```
1. task claim           ŌåÆ writes process/state.json (current task)
2. During execution     ŌåÆ write progress to process/record.json
3. task record --data   ŌåÆ reads JSON, generates records/*.md, clears process/
```

## JSON Data Format

```json
{
	"taskId": "3.3.1",
	"status": "completed",
	"summary": "What was implemented",
	"filesCreated": ["src/components/Button.tsx"],
	"filesModified": ["src/utils/helpers.ts"],
	"keyDecisions": ["Decision 1"],
	"testsPassed": 12,
	"testsFailed": 0,
	"coverage": 85.6,
	"acceptanceCriteria": [{ "criterion": "Acceptance criterion 1", "met": true }],
	"notes": "Optional observations"
}
```

## Fields

| Field                 | Type   | Description                                  |
| --------------------- | ------ | -------------------------------------------- |
| `taskId`              | string | Task ID (verified against CLI arg if provided) |
| `status`              | string | Task status, defaults to `completed`         |
| `summary`             | string | Implementation summary                       |
| `filesCreated`        | array  | List of newly created files                  |
| `filesModified`       | array  | List of modified files                       |
| `keyDecisions`        | array  | Key design decisions                         |
| `testsPassed`         | int    | Number of tests passed                       |
| `testsFailed`         | int    | Number of tests failed                       |
| `coverage`            | float  | Coverage percentage (collected from test runner) |
| `acceptanceCriteria`  | array  | `{criterion, met}` objects                   |
| `notes`               | string | Optional notes or observations               |

## Metrics Collection (MANDATORY before writing record.json)

<HARD-RULE>
Before writing `record.json`, you MUST collect real metrics from the project's test runner. All numeric fields (`coverage`, `testsPassed`, `testsFailed`) must come from actual output, never guessed or defaulted.

Coverage rules:

- `coverage` = actual percentage from test runner output
- `coverage` = `-1.0` is auto-set by CLI for tasks with `noTest: true`. For non-noTest tasks, always report real metrics.
- Never write `0.0` unless the runner actually reported 0%

Example commands (use whatever matches the project's toolchain):

```
just test [scope]
```

</HARD-RULE>

## Usage

```bash
# Step 1: Write progress to process/record.json (replace sample values with real metrics from above)
echo '{"taskId":"3.3.1","status":"completed","summary":"...","filesCreated":["..."],"filesModified":["..."],"keyDecisions":["..."],"testsPassed":12,"testsFailed":0,"coverage":85.6,"acceptanceCriteria":[{"criterion":"...","met":true}]}' > docs/features/{slug}/tasks/process/record.json

# Step 2: Use CLI command (mandatory)
task record <TASK_ID> --data docs/features/{slug}/tasks/process/record.json
```

<EXTREMELY-IMPORTANT>
You MUST use the `task record` CLI command. No exceptions.

**ONLY ALLOWED PATH:** `docs/features/{slug}/tasks/process/record.json`

**DO NOT:**

- Write directly to index.json
- Use Python/JavaScript to modify JSON
- Create record files manually
- Use Bash echo/cat to write JSON
- Think "both approaches achieve the same result"
- Use any other file path (e.g., .claude/tmp/)

The CLI command provides schema validation, consistent output format, and potential hooks/side-effects.
Bypassing the command defeats the purpose of the skill.
</EXTREMELY-IMPORTANT>

## What `task record` Does (One Command = 2 Operations)

```
task record <TASK_ID> --data docs/features/{slug}/tasks/process/record.json
```

This single command automatically:

1. Ō£ģ Generates `records/*.md` from JSON
2. Ō£ģ Updates `index.json` status

After running, check the STATUS field in the output:
- `STATUS: completed` ŌåÆ task recorded successfully, proceed to commit
- `STATUS: blocked` ŌåÆ task was auto-downgraded (e.g. test failures), **do NOT commit**

## Validation Rules (enforced by CLI)

### Quality Gate Pre-check

When `status=completed`, `--force` is NOT used, and the task does NOT have `noTest: true` in its index.json entry, `task record` automatically runs the full quality gate before accepting the record:

```
just compile [scope] ŌåÆ just fmt [scope] ŌåÆ just lint [scope] ŌåÆ just test [scope]
```

This gate runs via `validateQualityGate()` in record.go. If any step fails, the record is rejected with an error. Fix the errors and re-run `task record`, or use `--force` to bypass.

### Data Validation

`task record` will reject the following combinations:

| Condition | Error | Fix |
|-----------|-------|-----|
| `status=completed` + `testsFailed > 0` | Auto-downgrade to `blocked` (**non-overridable**) | Fix the test failures, then re-record with `status: "completed"` |
| `status=completed` + `testsPassed=0` + `testsFailed=0` + `coverage >= 0` | No test evidence (overridable) | Ensure task has `noTest: true` (CLI auto-sets coverage), or run tests and report results |
| `status=completed` + any `acceptanceCriteria.met=false` | Unmet acceptance criteria (overridable) | Fix the issue, or set `status: "blocked"` |
| `summary` is empty or whitespace | Missing summary | Provide a summary |

Override quality gate, test evidence, and AC validation with `--force`:
```bash
task record <TASK_ID> --data record.json --force
```

Note: auto-downgrade (`completed` + `testsFailed > 0` ŌåÆ `blocked`) is **never** overridden by `--force`. Fix the failing tests first.
Use `--force` only when you have a specific reason (document it in `notes`).

## Forbidden Operations

<EXTREMELY-IMPORTANT>
These actions will corrupt task state:

| Operation                    | Why Forbidden                              |
| ---------------------------- | ------------------------------------------ |
| `Write("records/*.md")`      | Bypasses CLI validation and hooks          |
| Direct edit to `index.json`  | State becomes inconsistent                 |
| `task status <id> completed` | Only for recovery when `task record` fails |
| Writing to wrong path        | CLI only reads from `process/record.json`  |

</EXTREMELY-IMPORTANT>

## Recovery (Only when `task record` fails)

If `task record` fails and cannot be fixed:

```bash
# Manual status fix (last resort only)
task status <TASK_ID> completed --force
```

# Evaluate e2e Test Cases

## Description

Call `/eval-test-cases` skill to evaluate test-cases.md for downstream executability.

## Reference Files

- `testing/test-cases.md` ŌĆö Test case document (generated by T-test-1)
- `prd/prd-spec.md` ŌĆö PRD spec
- `prd/prd-user-stories.md` ŌĆö User stories

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `testing/eval/report.md` exists with final score
- [ ] Final score >= 90

## Implementation Notes

1. Run `/eval-test-cases` skill with `--target 90 --iterations 6`
2. If target not reached after 6 iterations, mark this task as blocked

## Main Session Instructions

1. **Invoke skill**: `Skill(skill="forge:eval-test-cases", args="dashboard-custom-tools")`
2. **Check outcome**: Read `docs/features/dashboard-custom-tools/testing/eval/report.md`
3. **Record result**: Target reached ŌåÆ completed; NOT reached ŌåÆ blocked

content.content: Launching skill: forge:eval-test-cases

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /Users/fanhuifeng/.claude/plugins/cache/forge/forge/2.18.0/skills/eval-test-cases

# Eval Test Cases

## Prerequisites

Check previous stage artifacts. Abort and prompt user if missing:

| Artifact | Missing prompt |
|----------|----------------|
| `testing/test-cases.md` | Run `/gen-test-cases` first |
| `prd/prd-spec.md` | Run `/write-prd` first |
| `prd/prd-user-stories.md` | Run `/write-prd` first |

## When to Use

**Trigger:**
- User asks to "evaluate test cases" or "check test case quality"
- User provides `/eval-test-cases` command
- Before handing off test-cases.md to `/gen-test-scripts`
- Automatically via T-test-1b in the task chain

**Skip:**
- test-cases.md doesn't exist yet (use `/gen-test-cases` first)

## Parameters

| Parameter      | Default | Description                                           |
| -------------- | ------- | ----------------------------------------------------- |
| `--target`     | 90      | Target score (0-100). Loop stops when score >= target |
| `--iterations` | 6       | Max adversarial iterations                            |

## Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start]) --> B["1. Score (subagent)"]
    B --> C{"2. Gate (main session)"}
    C -->|"score >= target"| E(["Final Report Ō£ģ"])
    C -->|"score < target no iterations left"| F(["Final Report ŌØī"])
    C -->|"score < target iterations remaining"| D["3. Revise (subagent)"]
    D --> B

```

## Orchestrator Iron Laws

<EXTREMELY-IMPORTANT>
1. Main session controls the loop ŌĆö NEVER delegate the entire eval to a single agent
2. Only 3 actions per iteration: score ŌåÆ gate ŌåÆ revise
3. Gate (Step 3) runs in main session ŌĆö never inside a subagent
4. `--target` / `--iterations` are meaningless unless main session owns the loop
5. Scorer and reviser are independent subagents ŌĆö invoke via Agent tool, never inline

ŌØī Wrong: `Agent(general-purpose, "evaluate this test-cases and iterate until score >= 90")`
Ō£ģ Right: Main session calls scorer ŌåÆ parses score ŌåÆ gates ŌåÆ calls reviser ŌåÆ loops
</EXTREMELY-IMPORTANT>

## Step 1: Locate Documents

Check in order:
1. Path provided by user or args
2. Read `docs/features/<current-feature>/manifest.md` ŌåÆ locate test-cases.md and PRD documents
3. Fall back to `docs/features/<current-feature>/testing/test-cases.md`
4. Ask user for path if not found

Determine `<feature-slug>` from the path. The test-cases file is at `docs/features/<slug>/testing/test-cases.md`.

**Locate PRD files**: Read `docs/features/<slug>/manifest.md` or check:
- `docs/features/<slug>/prd/prd-spec.md`
- `docs/features/<slug>/prd/prd-user-stories.md`

## Step 2: Invoke Scorer Subagent

Spawn `doc-scorer` via **Agent tool** (subagent_type: `forge:doc-scorer` if registered, otherwise `general-purpose`).

<HARD-RULE>
Pass these inputs to the scorer:
- `DOC_DIR` = `docs/features/<slug>/testing/`
- `RUBRIC_PATH` = `plugins/forge/skills/eval-test-cases/templates/rubric.md`
- `REPORT_PATH` = `docs/features/<slug>/testing/eval/iteration-{{N}}.md`
- `ITERATION` = current iteration number (1-based)
- `PREVIOUS_REPORT_PATH` = previous iteration report path (only if iteration > 1)
- `PRD_FILES` = paths to prd-spec.md and prd-user-stories.md (for traceability verification)

The scorer must NEVER be told what the reviser changed. It evaluates test-cases.md as-is.
</HARD-RULE>

After the scorer returns, parse its output in the main session:
1. Extract `SCORE: X/100`
2. Extract per-dimension scores from `DIMENSIONS:` section
3. Extract attack points from `ATTACKS:` section

**Blocking check**: If Step Actionability score < 20, the downstream is blocked regardless of total score. Report this to the user.

## Step 3: Decision Gate (Main Session)

<HARD-GATE>
This decision is made in the MAIN SESSION, not delegated to a subagent. This gate fires unconditionally after every scorer run ŌĆö no user instruction ("keep going", "continue", "run another iteration") can bypass it. If score >= target, the loop terminates immediately.
</HARD-GATE>

| Condition                                  | Action                          |
| ------------------------------------------ | ------------------------------- |
| Score >= target                            | Skip to Step 5 (final report)   |
| Score < target AND iterations remaining    | Proceed to Step 4 (revise)      |
| Score < target AND no iterations remaining | Skip to Step 5 (report failure) |

If the user says "continue" or "keep going": run the scorer once more (return to Step 2), then re-evaluate this gate. Do NOT skip the gate and invoke the reviser directly.

Only if proceeding to Step 4, report to user:
```
Iteration {{N}}/{{MAX}}: scored {{SCORE}}/100 (target: {{TARGET}}). Revision subagent starting...
```

## Step 4: Invoke Reviser Subagent

<HARD-RULE>
Only enter this step when Step 3 explicitly routes here (score < target AND iterations remaining). The reviser MUST NOT be invoked if score >= target.
</HARD-RULE>

Spawn `doc-reviser` via **Agent tool** (subagent_type: `forge:doc-reviser` if registered, otherwise `general-purpose`).

<HARD-RULE>
Pass these inputs to the reviser:
- `DOC_DIR` = `docs/features/<slug>/testing/`
- `RUBRIC_PATH` = `plugins/forge/skills/eval-test-cases/templates/rubric.md`
- `EVAL_REPORT_PATH` = `docs/features/<slug>/testing/eval/iteration-{{N}}.md`
- `ATTACK_POINTS` = the 3 attack points extracted from scorer output

**Reviser scope**: ONLY modify `test-cases.md`. Do NOT modify PRD files or any other documents.
</HARD-RULE>

Increment iteration counter. Return to Step 2.

## Step 5: Final Report (Main Session)

```
## Eval-Test-Cases Complete

**Final Score**: {{SCORE}}/100 (target: {{TARGET}})
**Iterations Used**: {{N}}/{{MAX}}

### Score Progression
| Iteration | Score | Delta |
|-----------|-------|-------|
| 1 | {{s1}} | - |
| 2 | {{s2}} | +{{d2}} |

### Dimension Breakdown (final)
| Dimension | Score | Max |
|-----------|-------|-----|
| PRD Traceability | {{d1}} | 25 |
| Step Actionability | {{d2}} | 25 |
| Route & Element Accuracy | {{d3}} | 20 |
| Completeness | {{d4}} | 20 |
| Structure & ID Integrity | {{d5}} | 10 |

### Outcome
{{"Target reached" / "Target NOT reached ŌĆö N iterations exhausted"}}
{{If Step Actionability < 20: "ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ Step Actionability below blocking threshold ŌĆö downstream gen-test-scripts may fail"}}
{{If not reached: "Largest gaps: [dimension names]. Consider manual revision or increasing iterations."}}
```

Save the final report to `docs/features/<slug>/testing/eval/report.md`.

## Step 6: Next Step

After final report, ask via `AskUserQuestion`:

> Proceed to next phase?

- **Generate Test Scripts** ŌåÆ invoke `/gen-test-scripts` via `Skill` tool
- **No** ŌåÆ done


ARGUMENTS: dashboard-custom-tools

---
id: "T-test-2"
title: "Generate e2e Test Scripts"
priority: "P1"
estimated_time: "1-2h"
dependencies: ["T-test-1b"]
status: pending
noTest: false
mainSession: false
---

# Generate e2e Test Scripts

## Description

Call `/gen-test-scripts` skill to generate executable TypeScript e2e test scripts from test cases.

## Reference Files

- `testing/test-cases.md` ŌĆö Test case document
- `docs/sitemap/sitemap.json` ŌĆö Page element locators (if available)

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `tests/e2e/features/dashboard-custom-tools/` contains at least one spec file
- [ ] Each test() includes traceability comment `// Traceability: TC-NNN ŌåÆ {PRD Source}`
- [ ] TypeScript compilation passes: `cd tests/e2e && npx tsc --noEmit`

## Implementation Notes

1. Run `/gen-test-scripts` skill
2. Verify spec files exist under `tests/e2e/features/dashboard-custom-tools/`
3. Run TypeScript compilation check

Base directory for this skill: /Users/fanhuifeng/.claude/plugins/cache/forge/forge/2.18.0/skills/forensic # Forensic Agent Deviation Analysis ## When to Use **Trigger:** - User reports a skill/agent repeatedly deviating from expected behavior - User asks "why did the agent do X" about a past session - User wants to compare agent behavior across sessions for a specific skill - User provides `/forensic` command with a search term **Skip:** - Single-session post-mortem (use `/learn-lesson` instead) - Current-session debugging (investigate directly) ## Parameters | Parameter | Default | Description | | ------------- | ------- | ---------------------------------------------- | | `--keyword` | ŌĆö | Search history.jsonl for sessions with keyword | | `--session` | ŌĆö | Analyze a specific session ID | | `--skill` | ŌĆö | Analyze sessions that invoked a specific skill | | `--last` | 10 | Limit number of sessions to search | | `--target` | ŌĆö | Specific behavior to investigate (e.g. "agent ignored MAIN_SESSION flag") | ## Prerequisites `task` CLI must be installed with forensic subcommand (v2.15.0+). Verify: ```bash task forensic --help ``` If missing, build and install: `cd task-cli && go build -o ~/.zcode-task-cli/task ./cmd/task/` ## Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A["1. Search
history.jsonl"] --> B["2. Extract
evidence JSON"] B --> C["3. Load
skill definitions"] C --> D["4. Analyze
deviations"] D --> E["5. Report
docs/forensics/"] B --> B1["subagents
transcripts"] ``` ## Workflow After completing each workflow step, report its elapsed time in the format `[Step N: ]`. This ensures the user can see progress even when analysis takes several minutes. Example output after each step: ``` [Step 1: 3.2s] Found 4 sessions matching "init-justfile" [Step 2: 12.1s] Extracted evidence from 2 sessions (session-abc123: 8.4s, session-def456: 3.7s) [Step 3: 0.8s] Loaded 3 skill definitions [Step 4: 45.3s] Analyzed deviations across 2 sessions [Step 5: 1.2s] Report written to docs/forensics/init-justfile-deviation/report.md ``` ### Step 1: Locate Target Sessions Search `~/.claude/history.jsonl` to find relevant sessions. ```bash task forensic search "coding-harness/forge" --keyword "" --last ``` Or search by skill name: ```bash task forensic search "coding-harness/forge" --skill "" --last ``` From the results, identify sessions of interest. Read the `firstMsg` and `dateTime` to select relevant sessions. Present the session list to the user and confirm which sessions to analyze. ### Step 2: Extract Evidence For each confirmed session, extract compact evidence: ```bash # Derive JSONL path from sessionId # Path pattern: ~/.claude/projects/-Users-fanhuifeng-Projects-ai-coding-harness-forge/.jsonl mkdir -p docs/forensics//evidence task forensic extract ~/.claude/projects/-Users-fanhuifeng-Projects-ai-coding-harness-forge/.jsonl --out docs/forensics//evidence ``` Then check for subagent transcripts: ```bash task forensic subagents ~/.claude/projects/-Users-fanhuifeng-Projects-ai-coding-harness-forge/ ``` If subagents exist, extract their evidence too: ```bash task forensic extract --out docs/forensics//evidence ``` Evidence files are intermediate artifacts. Each extract produces ~10-20KB regardless of original JSONL size. Always use `--out` to write to the evidence directory ŌĆö never dump raw JSONL to terminal. ### Step 3: Load Expected Behavior From the evidence's `skillsUsed` field (or user's `--skill` parameter), read the relevant skill definitions: ``` plugins/forge/skills//SKILL.md ``` Extract the rules that the agent should have followed: - `` blocks ŌĆö mandatory constraints - `` blocks ŌĆö top-priority rules - `` blocks ŌĆö forbidden actions - Step-by-step workflow ŌĆö expected behavior sequence If `skillsUsed` is empty in the evidence (user messages may not always trigger skill detection), ask the user which skills were involved, or infer from the `--skill` parameter and the tool call patterns in the evidence. ### Step 4: Analyze Deviations Read the extracted evidence JSON from `docs/forensics//evidence/evidence.json`. **Proactively display timing data from the evidence.** The `summary.topSlowest` and `summary.timingByTool` fields contain per-action timing. When analyzing a session where the agent appeared stuck or inactive, present the timing breakdown prominently: ``` ## Timing Breakdown (session-abc123) | Action | Duration | Detail | |--------|----------|--------| | Agent (doc-scorer) | 142.3s | eval-design scoring... | | Read | 0.8s | plugins/forge/skills/... | | Bash | 45.2s | go test -race -cover ./... | | Edit | 0.3s | internal/cmd/forensic.go | Total tool time: 188.6s / Session duration: 12.4min ``` This helps the user immediately see where the agent spent time ŌĆö especially when investigating "stuck" tasks where one long-running tool call (often Agent/Bash) dominates. For each session, trace through: 1. **Thinking chain** ŌĆö Read each thinking block in order. Identify where reasoning diverges from the skill definition's expected workflow. 2. **Tool call sequence** ŌĆö Compare the actual tool calls against what the skill steps prescribe. Flag: - Tools called out of prescribed order - Tools called that aren't in the workflow - Required tools that were skipped 3. **Decision points** ŌĆö Where the thinking block shows a choice was made, check if the skill definition prescribes a specific choice. Flag deviations. Trace the causal chain at least 3 levels deep: 1. Symptom: What went wrong (observable behavior) 2. Direct cause: Which specific action/decision caused it 3. Root cause: Why the agent made that decision (instruction gap, context missing, wrong assumption) **Deviation categories** (use these to classify each finding): | Category | Description | Example | |----------|-------------|---------| | `instruction-gap` | Skill definition missing a critical rule | No instruction to handle MAIN_SESSION flag | | `context-starvation` | Agent lacked necessary information | Agent didn't see the record.json content | | `trust-without-verify` | Agent trusted its own output | Marked AC as met without running the artifact | | `wrong-priority` | Agent followed wrong priority | Chose "efficiency" over "safety" | | `scope-creep` | Agent exceeded its defined scope | Task executor claimed multiple tasks | | `pipeline-gap` | No enforcement between stages | Dispatcher checked file existence, not content | ### Step 5: Generate Report Write the forensic report using the template at `plugins/forge/skills/forensic/templates/report.md`. Output to: `docs/forensics//report.md` Present the report to the user. Do NOT commit automatically ŌĆö forensic reports are analysis artifacts that require human review. ## Common Mistakes - **Don't read raw JSONL files** ŌĆö Always use `task forensic extract` to compress the data. Raw JSONL is too large for analysis. - **Don't analyze a single data point** ŌĆö Cross-reference thinking blocks with the corresponding tool calls to understand the full decision chain. - **Don't skip the skill definition** ŌĆö You cannot identify deviations without knowing what the expected behavior was. - **Don't confuse symptom with root cause** ŌĆö "Agent recorded failed task as completed" is a symptom. "CLI validation accepts completed+testsFailed>0" is the root cause. ARGUMENTS: õĖ║õ╗Ćõ╣łstop hookĶ┐ÉĶĪīõ║å25ÕłåķÆ¤µ▓Īµ£ēń╗ōµØ¤
